---
title: "The Bloch Sphere"
description: >
  A geometric picture of a single qubit's pure-state space, with the math
  that backs the picture.
author: "John Golden"
date: "2026-05-23"
categories: [quantum-computing, qubits, geometry]
image: ../../figures/bloch_sphere.svg
---

A single qubit is the smallest interesting object in quantum information.
Despite that, its pure-state space already has a satisfying geometric
picture: the **Bloch sphere**. This post walks through where the sphere
comes from, fixes notation, and shows the canonical figure rendered by a
standalone script in the repo so the picture and the math stay in sync.

## From state vector to sphere {#sec-state-to-sphere}

A pure state of a qubit is a unit vector in $\mathbb{C}^2$, conventionally
written in the computational basis as

$$
|\psi\rangle \;=\; \alpha\, |0\rangle \;+\; \beta\, |1\rangle,
\qquad |\alpha|^2 + |\beta|^2 = 1.
$$ {#eq-state}

Equation [-@eq-state] has four real parameters reduced to three by the
normalization constraint. A further global phase is physically
irrelevant — multiplying $|\psi\rangle$ by $e^{i\gamma}$ leaves every
expectation value unchanged — which removes one more parameter. We are
left with **two real degrees of freedom**, which is exactly the dimension
of a sphere.

Concretely, pick the global phase so $\alpha$ is real and non-negative,
and parametrize

$$
|\psi(\theta, \phi)\rangle \;=\; \cos\!\tfrac{\theta}{2}\, |0\rangle
  \;+\; e^{i\phi}\sin\!\tfrac{\theta}{2}\, |1\rangle,
\qquad \theta \in [0,\pi],\; \phi \in [0, 2\pi).
$$ {#eq-bloch-parametrization}

The map $|\psi\rangle \mapsto (\theta, \phi)$ identifies the qubit's
ray space with the unit sphere $S^2 \subset \mathbb{R}^3$, with
Cartesian coordinates

$$
\vec{r} \;=\; \big(\sin\theta\cos\phi,\; \sin\theta\sin\phi,\; \cos\theta\big).
$$ {#eq-bloch-vector}

The vector $\vec{r}$ in @eq-bloch-vector is the **Bloch vector**. The
relationship between expectation values of the Pauli operators and
$\vec{r}$ is the key practical fact:
$\langle\sigma_x\rangle = r_x$, $\langle\sigma_y\rangle = r_y$, and
$\langle\sigma_z\rangle = r_z$.

The picture in @fig-bloch shows a generic pure state at
$(\theta, \phi) = (\pi/3, \pi/4)$.

![A qubit pure state $|\psi\rangle$ on the Bloch sphere. The computational
basis states $|0\rangle$ and $|1\rangle$ sit at the north and south poles.
Rendered by [`code/bloch_sphere.py`](../../code/bloch_sphere.py).](../../figures/bloch_sphere.svg){#fig-bloch width=70%}

## A small code snippet

The Bloch coordinates in @eq-bloch-vector are a one-liner. The snippet
below is **inert** — it is shown for reading, not executed at build
time — and matches the corresponding helper in the script that produces
@fig-bloch:

```python
def bloch_vector(theta: float, phi: float) -> np.ndarray:
    return np.array([
        np.sin(theta) * np.cos(phi),
        np.sin(theta) * np.sin(phi),
        np.cos(theta),
    ])
```

If you want to tweak the rendering, the full script lives at
[`code/bloch_sphere.py`](../../code/bloch_sphere.py); run `make figures`
from the repo root to regenerate every plot on the site.

## Mixed states and the ball

Pure states fill the surface of the sphere. **Mixed states** — convex
combinations of pure states — fill the interior, giving the Bloch
*ball*. Any single-qubit density matrix can be written as

$$
\rho \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left( I + \vec{r}\cdot \vec{\sigma}\right),
\qquad |\vec{r}\,| \le 1,
$$ {#eq-density}

with $|\vec{r}\,|=1$ recovering the pure-state surface and
$\vec{r} = 0$ giving the maximally mixed state $\rho = I/2$ at the
center. The geometric content of @eq-density is the cleanest first
example of how density matrices generalize state vectors.

## Where to go next

The Bloch sphere is the linchpin for single-qubit gate intuition:
unitary gates act as rotations of the sphere, decoherence shrinks
$|\vec{r}\,|$ toward the center, and measurement collapses
$|\psi\rangle$ onto a chosen axis. For the canonical treatment of all
three, see Chapter 1 of @nielsen_chuang_2010; the sphere itself first
appeared in @bloch_1946 as a tool for nuclear magnetic resonance, well
before the language of qubits existed.

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
